2018 · ICML 2018 · Post-training & RL

Addressing Function Approximation Error in Actor-Critic Methods

DDPG's deterministic actor-critic loop overestimates its own value function the same way tabular Q-learning does — TD3 fixes it with a clipped twin critic, delayed policy updates, and noised target actions, then became the default MuJoCo baseline reported next to SAC in nearly every continuous-control paper since.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Scott Fujimoto, Herke van Hoof, David Meger
venue ICML 2018 citations 7,113 topic Post-training & RL source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Overestimation bias is old news in tabular Q-learning: taking a max over noisy value estimates is itself a biased estimator, so the max propagates upward through every Bellman backup and inflates learned values well past the true return (Thrun & Schwartz, 1993). In the discrete-action setting this is well studied and half-fixed — Double Q-learning and Double DQN decouple the action that gets selected from the value that scores it, using two estimators so neither one's own error can be self-confirming. Nobody had shown whether the same failure shows up in actor-critic methods for continuous control, where the policy is a deterministic function climbing the critic's gradient rather than an explicit argmax.

The paper's first contribution is exactly that proof: under a deterministic policy gradient, the actor's update is itself biased toward actions the critic currently overvalues, so the same overestimation mechanism reappears. The authors show it empirically too — plotting DDPG's learned value estimate on Hopper-v1 and Walker2d-v1 against a Monte-Carlo estimate of the true value, DDPG's curve runs well above the true one for the entire million-step run (Figure 1). Their second, more surprising finding is that the field's standard discrete-action remedy doesn't transfer cleanly: adapting Double DQN's actor-critic analogue — using the current network as one estimator and the slow-moving target network as the other — barely helps, because with a slow Polyak-averaged policy the current and target actors stay too close together to give a genuinely independent second opinion. The standard fix, ported naively into DDPG's setting, quietly fails, and until this paper nobody had built a working substitute or even confirmed the underlying disease was the same one.

// how it works

The method

TD3 — Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic policy gradient — keeps DDPG's actor-critic skeleton (a deterministic actor πφ trained off-policy by climbing a learned critic's gradient, with a replay buffer and Polyak-averaged target networks) and bolts on three independent fixes, each aimed at a different half of the overestimation/variance problem.

Clipped Double Q-learning. Instead of DDPG's single critic Qθ, TD3 trains a pair of critics Qθ1, Qθ2 from the same replay data, and builds the Bellman target from whichever gives the smaller value (Equation 15 below). Because the target is a minimum over two estimators rather than a single noisy value, it can no longer be systematically pushed upward the way an unclipped max can; at worst it converts overestimation into mild underestimation, which the authors argue is the safer failure mode since underestimated actions are simply not favored by the policy rather than being chased and re-amplified. A fully faithful Double Q-learning would need two independently-updated actors, one per critic; for efficiency TD3 uses a single shared actor optimized against Qθ1 and folds Qθ2 in only as the clipping partner.

Delayed policy updates. The actor and both target networks are updated only once every d = 2 critic updates (Algorithm 1). The critic trains every step; the policy waits. Updating the actor against an under-converged, high-variance critic compounds whatever error is in that estimate into the policy — and that error then feeds back into the next round of Bellman targets, since the actor's actions are what the critic bootstraps from at the next state. Slowing the actor down gives the critic more time to settle before the policy chases it, at the direct cost of halving how often the actor is ever updated over a fixed number of environment steps.

Target policy smoothing. A deterministic target policy lets the critic overfit to narrow, spurious peaks in the value landscape — a sharp Q-value at one action with no support from nearby actions is often noise, not signal. TD3 regularizes against this the way SARSA implicitly does, fitting the value of a small neighborhood around the target action rather than the single point: clipped Gaussian noise is added to the target action before it is fed to the target critics.

y = r + γ mini=1,2 Qθ'ᵢ(s′, πφ′(s′) + ε) // ε ~ clip(N(0, σ̃), −c, c) — clipped Double Q-learning target with target policy smoothing (Eq. 15); the min over two critics limits overestimation, the additive clipped noise smooths the target over nearby actions
// what they found

The results

TD3 is evaluated against DDPG, a hyperparameter-matched "Our DDPG" control (same network sizes, Adam optimizer, and exploration noise as TD3 — a fairness check the original DDPG hyperparameters didn't get), PPO, TRPO, ACKTR, and SAC, across seven OpenAI Gym MuJoCo-v1 tasks, each run for 1M environment steps over 10 random seeds (Figure 5, Table 1). Margins on the harder locomotion tasks are large: TD3 reaches a max average return of 9636.95 ± 859.065 on HalfCheetah (next best: Our DDPG at 8577.29), 4682.82 ± 539.64 on Walker2d (next best: PPO at 3317.69), and 4372.44 ± 1000.33 on Ant (next best: ACKTR at 1821.94) — roughly 2-4x every baseline, including the authors' own re-tuned DDPG. On Hopper, TD3 posts 3564.07 ± 114.74, ahead of SAC's 2996.66 and TRPO's 2471.30.

On the two easier tasks the margin nearly vanishes. InvertedPendulum is saturated — every method except TRPO hits the 1000.00 ceiling. On Reacher (TD3: -3.60 ± 0.56) and InvertedDoublePendulum (TD3: 9337.47 ± 14.96), TD3's own error bars overlap the best baseline (Our DDPG's -4.01 on Reacher; original DDPG's 9355.52 on InvertedDoublePendulum) — Table 1 bolds both entries as joint-best in each case, a tacit acknowledgment that the abstract's claim of "outperforming the state of the art in every environment tested" is a clean win on 5 of 7 tasks and a statistical tie on the other 2.

Ant4372.44 ± 1000.331005.30888.771083.20-75.851821.94655.35
Reacher-3.60 ± 0.56-6.51-4.01-6.18-111.43-4.26-4.44
InvPendulum1000.00 ± 0.001000.001000.001000.00985.401000.001000.00

Table 1. Max average return over 10 trials of 1M time steps; ± is one standard deviation over trials. Bold = maximum (or statistical tie for maximum) per row, as in the original paper.

The ablation in Table 2 is as informative as the headline numbers. Removing clipped double-Q from the full method ("TD3 − CDQ") costs almost nothing on HalfCheetah (9792.80, actually higher than full TD3's 9532.99 in the ablation run) but collapses Ant from 4185.06 to 849.75 and Walker2d from 4565.24 to 2579.39 — the component that gives the method its name is only load-bearing on some tasks. Delayed policy updates and target policy smoothing show the opposite pattern (each helps a little almost everywhere and hurts nowhere badly); only the full combination is consistently at or near the top across all four ablated tasks.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

TD3 is itself a response to a specific reproducibility complaint: Henderson et al. (2017), cited explicitly in the introduction, had shown that headline deep-RL gains were often not attributable to the claimed algorithmic change once seed variance, unreported hyperparameters, and implementation quirks were accounted for. The authors respond in kind — 10 seeds per task, a hyperparameter-matched "Our DDPG" control that isolates the algorithmic contribution from architecture/optimizer choices, and released code and learning-curve data — and that discipline is a large part of why the paper still holds up eight years later. But the paper's central move, clipping the critic target to the smaller of two Q-estimates, is a bias trade, not a bias elimination: it swaps a well-characterized overestimation failure (which the paper shows gets chased and amplified by the policy) for underestimation, on the argument that underestimation is "safer" because underestimated actions simply aren't selected rather than being re-amplified. That argument is plausible but not directly measured — there's no experiment quantifying how far below the true value the clipped estimate actually sits, the way Figure 1 measures DDPG's overestimation directly. The ablation makes the cost of that trade concrete: on HalfCheetah, removing clipped double-Q entirely ("TD3 − CDQ", 9792.80) beats the full method (9532.99) — the fix that gives the paper its name is not uniformly helping.

baselines

All six baselines (DDPG, Our DDPG, PPO, TRPO, ACKTR, SAC) are re-run by the authors under one common OpenAI Gym MuJoCo-v1 harness with 10 seeds each, rather than quoted from each baseline's own paper — a real strength. But "Our DDPG," the hyperparameter-matched re-implementation that most directly isolates TD3's algorithmic contribution, is the authors' own re-tuning of a competitor, and it happens to win or statistically tie 2 of the 7 tasks (Reacher, InvertedDoublePendulum). A comparison built by the method's own authors, however carefully matched, is still worth a raised eyebrow next to an independent replication. The paper also footnotes an unresolved "discrepancy" between the SAC numbers it reports and SAC's own concurrent paper, without reconciling it in the main text.

contamination

Not applicable in the leakage/test-set sense — this is online RL evaluated by interacting with a simulator, not scored against a fixed held-out set. The closer analogue is overfitting to a narrow evaluation protocol: all seven tasks are the original, unmodified OpenAI Gym MuJoCo-v1 suite, chosen explicitly "for reproducible comparison" (a genuine strength), but that suite is fully-observed, dense-reward, low-dimensional-action, and simulated — nothing here tests sparse reward, partial observability, sim-to-real transfer, or pixel observations (which DDPG's own paper tested and TD3 does not).

what's missed

Table 1's "max average return over 10 trials" reports each seed's single best evaluation checkpoint, not final or converged performance — a method that spikes once and decays would still score well by this metric, and there's no steps-to-reach-threshold sample-efficiency figure, which often matters more than final return under a fixed compute budget. The ablation (Table 2) is reported only on 4 of the 7 tasks (HalfCheetah, Hopper, Walker2d, Ant) — Reacher and the two pendulum tasks, exactly where TD3's margin over baselines was thinnest, get no component-level breakdown.

would raise confidence

A direct measurement of the clipped estimate's underestimation magnitude (the same Monte-Carlo-vs-estimate comparison Figure 1 already runs for DDPG, applied to CDQ itself); ablation results extended to all seven tasks rather than four; and evaluation on tasks with sparser reward or higher action dimensionality, where overestimation is believed to bite hardest and where a bias trade would show its cost most clearly.

// bottom line

TD3 earns its place as the default continuous-control baseline less because clipped double-Q is a fully justified fix — the ablation shows it can cost performance on the very task (HalfCheetah) where the margin over baselines is largest — than because the full package, plus the discipline of matched hyperparameters, 10 seeds, and a re-tuned DDPG control, set a new floor for how carefully a deep-RL paper evaluates itself, arriving just as the field was reckoning with exactly that problem.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Overestimation bias isn't a discrete-action artifact — it reappears under deterministic policy gradients too, and the field's standard fix (Double DQN's decoupled estimators) doesn't transfer cleanly, because a slow Polyak-averaged target policy stays too similar to the current one to be a genuinely independent second estimate.
  2. TD3's three components — clipped double-Q, delayed policy updates, and target policy smoothing — target different problems (bias, error accumulation, and variance/overfitting respectively), and the ablation shows they don't contribute equally on every task; clipped double-Q in particular can cost performance where overestimation wasn't the bottleneck.
  3. TD3 (alongside SAC) became the field's default MuJoCo baseline largely because of its evaluation discipline — 10 seeds, matched hyperparameters, a re-tuned baseline control, released learning curves — a direct, explicit response to Henderson et al.'s deep-RL reproducibility critique, not the algorithm alone.
